content.text: Anthropic recommends Voyage AI as the embedding provider for Claude-based retrieval systems. Voyage AI produces dense vector representations that are fine-tuned to pair well with Claude's representation of knowledge, yielding higher retrieval precision on RAG benchmarks than general-purpose embedding providers.

Voyage AI is a standalone embedding API that converts text — documents, queries, code, or any other UTF-8 string — into high-dimensional dense vectors. The vectors capture semantic meaning so that texts with similar content cluster together in vector space, enabling approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search to find relevant passages for a given query. Voyage AI offers a family of models tuned for different domains and latency/cost tradeoffs.

Anthropic's partnership with Voyage AI means that the Voyage models are specifically fine-tuned against Claude-generated data and Claude's semantic space. When Claude reads a retrieved passage, its internal representation of that passage aligns with the passage's Voyage embedding, reducing the semantic mismatch that can cause a retrieved document to feel irrelevant once Claude reads it. This alignment is the core reason Anthropic recommends Voyage over general-purpose alternatives like OpenAI text-embedding-3 or Cohere embed for Claude-centric RAG.

Retrieval quality is the ceiling on RAG quality. If the retriever does not surface the relevant passage, Claude cannot synthesize a correct answer regardless of its reasoning capability. Most RAG failures in production are retrieval failures, not generation failures. Choosing an embedding model fine-tuned to match Claude's internal representations directly raises that ceiling.

Voyage AI models also support long context windows (up to 32,000 tokens for voyage-3-large), which matters for legal, scientific, and financial documents where a single chunk may need to span several paragraphs to preserve logical context. General-purpose models capped at 512 or 1,024 tokens require aggressive chunking that can break semantic units, degrading retrieval.
Higher retrieval precision for Claude RAG pipelines compared to generic embedding models on standard benchmarks (MTEB, BEIR).
Domain-specific models for code (voyage-code-3) and general text (voyage-3-large) avoid the quality degradation that comes from applying a single model across mixed-domain corpora.
Long context window (32k tokens for voyage-3-large) supports embedding full sections or pages without truncation.
Simple REST API compatible with OpenAI's embeddings format — drop-in replacement requiring only endpoint and key changes.
Batch API supports up to 128 texts per request, reducing HTTP overhead on indexing pipelines.

Voyage AI is a paid third-party API. While Anthropic recommends it, using it adds an external dependency, a separate API key, and a separate billing relationship. Teams that have already standardized on another embedding provider (Cohere, Google, OpenAI) face migration cost and dual-vendor complexity. For most production Claude RAG deployments the retrieval quality improvement justifies the dependency, but for internal prototypes or low-stakes applications any reasonable embedding provider will do.

Voyage AI does not offer a self-hosted option. All embeddings are computed on Voyage infrastructure. This may be a compliance blocker for organizations with data residency requirements that prohibit sending document text to third-party APIs. In those cases, a locally hosted alternative (sentence-transformers, vLLM serving a BAAI/bge model) is the only option.

The Voyage API accepts an array of text strings and returns an array of floating-point vectors, one per input. Each vector has a fixed dimensionality determined by the model: voyage-3-large and voyage-code-3 produce 1,024-dimensional vectors; voyage-3-lite produces 512-dimensional vectors. The vectors are L2-normalized, so cosine similarity and dot product give identical rankings, enabling both inner-product and cosine ANN indices without renormalization.

The input_type parameter controls whether the text is encoded as a document (for indexing) or a query (for retrieval). The document and query encoders share weights but apply different learned biases so that asymmetric retrieval (short query vs. long document) works correctly. Always specify input_type='document' when indexing and input_type='query' when encoding search queries; omitting this parameter degrades retrieval quality by 3–10% depending on corpus.

attrs.alt: Voyage AI embedding pipeline architecture diagram
attrs.title: Voyage AI Embedding Pipeline

content.text: The diagram shows a two-phase pipeline. During indexing, documents are chunked, batched, and embedded via the Voyage API with input_type='document', then stored in a vector store. During query time, the user's question is embedded with input_type='query', and the vector store returns the top-K nearest chunks for Claude to synthesize.

Use Voyage AI as the embedding provider whenever Claude is the generation model and retrieval quality is important. This covers enterprise knowledge bases, customer support RAG, code search, and research-assistant pipelines where incorrect retrieval creates material errors.

Prefer voyage-code-3 specifically when the corpus contains significant code — function bodies, configuration files, SQL schemas. General text models like voyage-3-large embed code with lower discriminative resolution because their training data under-represents code. Use voyage-3-lite when query latency or embedding cost is the primary constraint and a small accuracy drop is acceptable.
Install the Voyage SDK and configure your API key. The SDK wraps the REST API with automatic retries and batching helpers.

import voyageai
from typing import Sequence

vo = voyageai.Client()  # reads VOYAGE_API_KEY from env

def embed_documents(texts: Sequence[str], model: str = "voyage-3-large") -> list[list[float]]:
    """Embed a batch of document chunks for indexing."""
    # Voyage accepts up to 128 texts and 120,000 tokens per batch
    batch_size = 64
    all_embeddings: list[list[float]] = []
    for i in range(0, len(texts), batch_size):
        batch = texts[i : i + batch_size]
        result = vo.embed(
            batch,
            model=model,
            input_type="document",  # IMPORTANT: always specify
        )
        all_embeddings.extend(result.embeddings)
    return all_embeddings

def embed_query(query: str, model: str = "voyage-3-large") -> list[float]:
    """Embed a single search query."""
    result = vo.embed([query], model=model, input_type="query")
    return result.embeddings[0]
Indexing a document corpus into pgvector

import voyageai, psycopg2, psycopg2.extras, uuid
from textwrap import wrap  # naive chunker for illustration

vo = voyageai.Client()
conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://localhost/ragdb")
psycopg2.extras.register_vector(conn)

CREATE_TABLE = """
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS doc_chunks (
    id         UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    source     TEXT,
    chunk_text TEXT,
    embedding  vector(1024)
);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS doc_chunks_embedding_idx
    ON doc_chunks USING ivfflat (embedding vector_cosine_ops) WITH (lists = 100);
"""

with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(CREATE_TABLE)
conn.commit()

# --- Indexing ---
documents = [
    {"source": "policy.txt", "text": "...full policy text..."},
    {"source": "faq.txt",    "text": "...full FAQ text..."},
]

chunks = []
for doc in documents:
    for chunk in wrap(doc["text"], 1800):  # ~512 tokens at 3.5 chars/tok
        chunks.append({"source": doc["source"], "text": chunk})

texts = [c["text"] for c in chunks]
embeddings = embed_documents(texts)  # from snippet above

with conn.cursor() as cur:
    psycopg2.extras.execute_values(
        cur,
        "INSERT INTO doc_chunks (source, chunk_text, embedding) VALUES %s",
        [(c["source"], c["text"], emb) for c, emb in zip(chunks, embeddings)],
        template="(%s, %s, %s::vector)",
    )
conn.commit()
print(f"Indexed {len(chunks)} chunks")

import anthropic, voyageai, psycopg2, psycopg2.extras

vo = voyageai.Client()
ac = anthropic.Anthropic()
conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://localhost/ragdb")
psycopg2.extras.register_vector(conn)

def retrieve(query: str, top_k: int = 5) -> list[str]:
    q_emb = embed_query(query)
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(
            """SELECT chunk_text
               FROM doc_chunks
               ORDER BY embedding <=> %s::vector
               LIMIT %s""",
            (q_emb, top_k),
        )
        return [row[0] for row in cur.fetchall()]

def rag_answer(question: str) -> str:
    passages = retrieve(question)
    context = "\n\n---\n\n".join(passages)
    response = ac.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"""Use the context below to answer the question.

Context:
{context}

Question: {question}"""
        }],
    )
    return response.content[0].text

print(rag_answer("What is the refund policy for digital products?"))

A legal technology company built a contract analysis pipeline over a corpus of 200,000 contracts (totalling ~4 billion tokens of text). Using voyage-3-large with 1,024-token chunks (approximately 3,500 characters with 10% overlap), they indexed the corpus into 1.6 million chunks. With voyage-3-large's 32k-token context window, long contract clauses that previously required mid-sentence truncation could be embedded whole, improving clause-boundary retrieval by 22% on their proprietary evaluation set.

For the query path they embed each natural-language query with input_type='query' and retrieve the top-20 chunks, passing the top-5 to Claude claude-opus-4-8 for synthesis. They compared Voyage AI to OpenAI text-embedding-3-large on 500 manually labeled query-passage pairs from their contract domain and measured 11.4 percentage points higher nDCG@5 with Voyage, which translated to a 9% reduction in Claude hallucination rate (measured by legal expert annotation).

Omitting input_type is the most common mistake. The Voyage API accepts 'document', 'query', and None; omitting it is equivalent to None, which applies a generic encoder that performs worse than either the document or query encoder on asymmetric retrieval tasks. Always pass input_type='document' at index time and input_type='query' at query time.

# WRONG: omit input_type
result = vo.embed(texts, model="voyage-3-large")

# RIGHT: specify input_type for every call
result = vo.embed(texts, model="voyage-3-large", input_type="document")  # indexing
result = vo.embed([query], model="voyage-3-large", input_type="query")   # retrieval

Mixing models between indexing and query time causes silent relevance collapse. The embedding spaces of different Voyage models are not aligned — voyage-3 document vectors are not comparable to voyage-3-large query vectors. Store the model name alongside each chunk and enforce that query embeddings use the same model as the indexed embeddings.
Always specify input_type — 'document' for indexing, 'query' for retrieval. This is the highest-impact parameter in the API.
Store the embedding model name in your vector store alongside each chunk — queries must use the same model as the indexed documents.
Batch in groups of 64 rather than 128 — the 128-text limit is a hard API cap; 64 provides headroom for large individual chunks without hitting token limits.
Use voyage-code-3 for code corpora and voyage-3-large for mixed text — the code model is fine-tuned to treat identifiers, operators, and comments as semantic units.
Cache query embeddings in Redis with a 1-hour TTL for frequently repeated queries — embedding calls add 20–80 ms to query latency; caching eliminates this for repeated searches.

Voyage AI's API processes batches of 128 texts in 200–500 ms for voyage-3-large (1,024-dim). For a corpus of 1 million chunks, that is approximately 7,800 API calls at batch size 128, taking 26–65 minutes at full throughput if calls are made sequentially. Parallelizing with asyncio or a thread pool across 8–16 concurrent requests reduces this to 3–8 minutes in practice.

import asyncio, voyageai
from typing import Sequence

vo = voyageai.AsyncClient()  # async client for concurrent requests

async def embed_batch(texts: list[str], model: str) -> list[list[float]]:
    result = await vo.embed(texts, model=model, input_type="document")
    return result.embeddings

async def embed_all(chunks: Sequence[str], model: str = "voyage-3-large",
                    batch_size: int = 64, concurrency: int = 8) -> list[list[float]]:
    batches = [chunks[i:i+batch_size] for i in range(0, len(chunks), batch_size)]
    sem = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency)
    results: list[list[list[float]]] = [None] * len(batches)

    async def process(i: int, batch: list[str]):
        async with sem:
            results[i] = await embed_batch(batch, model)

    await asyncio.gather(*[process(i, b) for i, b in enumerate(batches)])
    return [emb for batch_result in results for emb in batch_result]

The voyageai Python SDK (pip install voyageai) provides both synchronous and asynchronous clients. LangChain has a native VoyageAIEmbeddings class (langchain-community) that plugs into any LangChain retriever or document store. LlamaIndex has a VoyageEmbedding class in llama-index-embeddings-voyageai. Both integrations handle batching and input_type automatically when used through their respective framework pipelines.

Document text sent to the Voyage API is processed on Voyage infrastructure. Review Voyage's data processing agreement and data retention policy before sending confidential documents. Voyage's API does not train on customer data by default, but confirm this in the enterprise terms for sensitive use cases. If data must not leave your infrastructure, use a self-hosted model instead.

The Voyage API enforces rate limits (requests per minute and tokens per minute per tier). Index large corpora with exponential backoff and respect the Retry-After header on 429 responses. The voyageai SDK handles retries automatically with configurable max_retries and timeout parameters. For production pipelines, implement a circuit breaker that falls back to a cached embedding or a secondary provider if Voyage is unavailable.
Voyage AI is Anthropic's recommended embedding provider because its models are fine-tuned to align with Claude's semantic space, improving RAG retrieval precision for Claude-centric pipelines.
Always specify input_type='document' when indexing and input_type='query' when retrieving — this single parameter has the largest impact on retrieval quality.
Use voyage-3-large for general text and voyage-code-3 for code corpora; both produce 1,024-dimensional L2-normalized vectors suitable for cosine or dot-product ANN indices.
Batch in groups of 64, parallelize with asyncio for large corpora, and cache query embeddings for repeated searches.
Store the model name with each indexed chunk and enforce model consistency at query time — mixed-model retrieval silently degrades relevance.
For data residency requirements where document text cannot leave your infrastructure, use a locally hosted alternative (BAAI/bge models via sentence-transformers or vLLM).
